content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 12, 1990

Lance H. Olson
Olson, Connelly, Hagel, Fong & Leidigh
300 Capital Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-90-515

Dear Mr. Olson:

You have requested informal assistance concerning the
campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ Your letter
does not indicate that you are seeking advice on behalf of a
person who has duties under the Act. Accordingly, we consider
your request to be one for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

QUESTIONS

If a labor union makes contributions out of its general fund
to a ballot measure committee, may the labor union report these
contributions on its sponsored committee's campaign statement with
a notation that the expenditures were made from the sponsoring
organization's general fund? Would a separate major donor
statement be required if the contributions total $10,000 or more
in a calendar year and are reported as stated above? Would the
labor union be required to file as a recipient committee if it
made contributions in any prior calendar year out of its general
fund?

CONCLUSIONS

In the first calendar year in which the labor union makes
contributions out of its general fund, the labor union would not
have any reporting obligations unless it contributes $10,000 or

__________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Lance H. Olson
Page Two

more during the year. If the labor union's contributions during
the first year total $10,000 or more, the contributions may be
reported by either of the following methods:

     (1)  The labor union may disclose the contributions on a
"Major Donor And Independent Expenditure Committee Campaign
Statement" (Form 461); or

     (2)  The contributions may be disclosed on the campaign
disclosure statement (Form 420) filed by its sponsored committee.
In this case, the total amount of contributions made by the union
is itemized on Schedule A of the committee's Form 420; each
contribution is itemized on Schedule E, with a notation by each
contribution that it was made from the sponsor's general fund; and
each contribution is itemized on the Allocation Page of the
committee's Form 420.  A responsible officer of the union must
verify the committee's campaign statements.

     In any subsequent calendar year the union makes contributions
totaling $1,000 or more from the general fund, the labor union may
do either of the following:

     (1)  File a statement of organization (Form 410) within ten
days of contributing $1,000, thereby creating another sponsored
recipient committee, or

     (2)  Report contributions made by the labor union's general
fund on the existing sponsored recipient committee's campaign
statement.  The contributions must be reported in the manner set
out in (2) above. In addition, if any member's pro-rata share of
the contributions made (including the sponsored committee
contributions and the general fund contributions) is $100 or more,
the contribution must be itemized on Schedule A, including the
member's name, address, occupation, and employer.


                                    ANALYSIS

     Section 82013 defines a committee as "any person or
combination of persons who receives contributions totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year...., makes independent
expenditures of $1,000 or more in a calendar year, or makes
contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year to or at
the behest of candidates or committees."  A "contribution" is
defined to include any payment, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes.  (Section 82015.)

Lance H. Olson
Page Three

     Assuming that at the time the payments were received by the
general fund from labor union members, the labor union had never
made political contributions or expenditures from the general
fund, and had not informed its members that it intended to do so
in the future, the payments received were not made by the members
for political purposes.  Thus, the payments are not contributions.
(Gross Advice Letter, No. A-86-295, copy enclosed.)  Therefore,
the labor union would not qualify as a recipient committee under
Section 82013(a).

     The labor union would have no reporting requirements unless
it contributed $10,000 or more in one calendar year (Section
82013(c)), or made independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or
more in a calendar year (Section 82013(b)).

     Once the labor union has established a history of making
contributions from its general fund, its members are deemed to be
on notice in subsequent years that a portion of their payments may
be used for political purposes.  Thus, in any subsequent calendar
year in which the labor union makes contributions out of its
general fund totaling $1,000 or more, it will qualify as a
recipient committee under Section 82013(a).

     However, in the case of a labor union which already has a
sponsored committee, it has been the Commission's informal advice
that contributions made by the organization directly (i.e., from a
fund other than its sponsored committee's funds) may be disclosed
on the sponsored committee's campaign statements.  This is because
the union and its sponsored committee are considered, for purposes
of the Act's campaign disclosure provisions, a single contributor.
The sponsored committee, for this purpose, is treated as the
political arm of the union and, therefore, we believe the purpose
of public disclosure is best served if their contributions are
reported on a single campaign disclosure statement.  In addition,
the requirement that the name of the sponsor be included in the name
of its sponsored committee (Section 84102(a)), provides
information to the public concerning the relationship between the
sponsor and its committee.

     If you have additional questions, please call me at (916)
322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabryn
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By:  Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
                                             Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

Would a separate major donor statement be required if the contributions total $10,000 or more in a calendar year and are reported as stated above? Would the labor union be required to file as a recipient committee if it made contributions in any prior calendar year out of its general fund?

In the first calendar year in which the labor union makes contributions out of its general fund, the labor union would not have any reporting obligations unless it contributes $10,000 or more.

__________________________

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

more during the year. If the labor union's contributions during the first year total $10,000 or more, the contributions may be reported by either of the following methods:

(1) The labor union may disclose the contributions on a "Major Donor And Independent Expenditure Committee Campaign Statement" (Form 461); or

(2) The contributions may be disclosed on the campaign disclosure statement (Form 420) filed by its sponsored committee. In this case, the total amount of contributions made by the union is itemized on Schedule A of the committee's Form 420; each contribution is itemized on Schedule E, with a notation by each contribution that it was made from the sponsor's general fund; and each contribution is itemized on the Allocation Page of the committee's Form 420. A responsible officer of the union must verify the committee's campaign statements.

In any subsequent calendar year the union makes contributions totaling $1,000 or more from the general fund, the labor union may do either of the following:

(1) File a statement of organization (Form 410) within ten days of contributing $1,000, thereby creating another sponsored recipient committee, or

(2) Report contributions made by the labor union's general fund on the existing sponsored recipient committee's campaign statement. The contributions must be reported in the manner set out in (2) above. In addition, if any member's pro-rata share of the contributions made (including the sponsored committee contributions and the general fund contributions) is $100 or more, the contribution must be itemized on Schedule A, including the member's name, address, occupation, and employer.
sections.analysis:
Section 82013 defines a committee as "any person or combination of persons who ... receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year..., makes independent expenditures of $1,000 or more in a calendar year, or makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees." A "contribution" is defined to include any payment, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015.)

Assuming that at the time the payments were received by the general fund from labor union members, the labor union had never made political contributions or expenditures from the general fund, and had not informed its members that it intended to do so in the future, the payments received were not made by the members for political purposes. Thus, the payments are not contributions. (Gross Advice Letter, No. A-86-295, copy enclosed.) Therefore, the labor union would not qualify as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a).

The labor union would have no reporting requirements unless it contributed $10,000 or more in one calendar year (Section 82013(c)), or made independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year (Section 82013(b)).

Once the labor union has established a history of making contributions from its general fund, its members are deemed to be on notice in subsequent years that a portion of their payments may be used for political purposes. Thus, in any subsequent calendar year in which the labor union makes contributions out of its general fund totaling $1,000 or more, it will qualify as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a).

However, in the case of a labor union which already has a sponsored committee, it has been the Commission's informal advice that contributions made by the organization directly (i.e., from a fund other than its sponsored committee's funds) may be disclosed on the sponsored committee's campaign statements. This is because the union and its sponsored committee are considered, for purposes of the Act's campaign disclosure provisions, a single contributor. The sponsored committee, for this purpose, is treated as the political arm of the union and, therefore, we believe the purpose of public disclosure is best served if their contributions are reported on a single campaign disclosure statement. In addition, the requirement that the name of the sponsor be included in the name of its sponsored committee (Section 84102(a)), provides information to the public concerning the relationship between the sponsor and its committee.

QUESTION: If a labor union makes contributions out of its general fund to a ballot measure committee, may the labor union report these contributions on its sponsored committee’s campaign statement with a notation that the expenditures were made from the sponsoring organization’s general fund? Would a separate major donor statement be required if the contributions total $10,000 or more in a calendar year and are reported as stated above? Would the labor union be required to file as a recipient committee if it made contributions in any prior calendar year out of its general fund?

CONCLUSION: In the first calendar year in which the labor union makes contributions out of its general fund, the labor union would not have any reporting obligations unless it contributes $10,000 or more.

__________________________

more during the year. If the labor union's contributions during the first year total $10,000 or more, the contributions may be reported by either of the following methods:

(1) The labor union may disclose the contributions on a "Major Donor And Independent Expenditure Committee Campaign Statement" (Form 461); or

In this case, the total amount of contributions made by the union is itemized on Schedule A of the committee's Form 420; each contribution is itemized on Schedule E, with a notation by each contribution that it was made from the sponsor's general fund; and each contribution is itemized on the Allocation Page of the committee's Form 420. A responsible officer of the union must verify the committee's campaign statements.

In any subsequent calendar year the union makes contributions totaling $1,000 or more from the general fund, the labor union may do either of the following:

(1) File a statement of organization (Form 410) within ten days of contributing $1,000, thereby creating another sponsored recipient committee, or

The contributions must be reported in the manner set out in (2) above.
